cards.title: Behaviour Change — Transtheoretical Model
cards._legacyHtml:
5 Stages of Change (Prochaska & DiClemente):
1. Precontemplation: Not considering change. Strategy: raise awareness, provide information without pressure. Do not push action.
2. Contemplation: Aware of problem, ambivalent. Strategy: decisional balance (pros/cons of change). Explore ambivalence.
3. Preparation: Intending to act within 30 days. Strategy: help set specific SMART goal. Identify barriers and resources.
4. Action: Actively making change (0–6 months). Strategy: support, reinforce, problem-solve obstacles. Relapse plan in place.
5. Maintenance: Sustained change >6 months. Strategy: prevent relapse, identify high-risk situations, build self-efficacy.
Clinical application: Assess stage at every visit. Tailor intervention. Never push action-stage advice on precontemplators — creates reactance and dropout.

cards.title: Motivational Interviewing — OARS
cards._legacyHtml:
OARS — Core MI Skills:
O — Open-ended questions: 'What concerns you most about your health?' 'How would your life be different if you made this change?' Avoid yes/no questions.
A — Affirmations: Genuine recognition of patient strengths. 'You managed to walk 20 minutes despite your busy schedule — that shows real commitment.' Not generic praise.
R — Reflective listening: Simple reflection (repeat key words). Complex reflection (reflect emotion or meaning). 'It sounds like you feel frustrated that your body isn't responding the way you expect.'
S — Summaries: Collect-and-link technique. 'Let me make sure I understand what you've shared…' Summarise ambivalence, emphasise change talk.
Change talk (DARN-CAT): Desire, Ability, Reasons, Need (preparatory) → Commitment, Activation, Taking steps (mobilising). Reinforce change talk. Gently explore sustain talk without arguing.
Spirit of MI: Partnership, Acceptance, Compassion, Evocation.

cards.title: SMART Goals Framework
cards._legacyHtml:
S — Specific: 'I will walk' not 'I will exercise'. 'I will walk in my colony at 6:30 AM on Monday, Wednesday, Friday.'
M — Measurable: '30 minutes' not 'a while'. Include a tracking method — step counter, diary, app.
A — Achievable: Patient must believe the goal is possible for them now. Start small (10 min walk) rather than ideal (60 min). Build self-efficacy.
R — Relevant: Linked to patient's own values and priorities. 'I want to play with my grandchildren' not 'doctor says to exercise'.
T — Time-bound: 'For the next 2 weeks' gives a review point. Not open-ended.
Example SMART goal: 'I will walk for 20 minutes after my evening meal, 5 days a week, starting Monday, and track it in my notebook for 2 weeks.'
Confidence scaling: 'On a scale of 0–10, how confident are you that you can do this?' If <7, simplify the goal. Ask 'What would make it a 7?'

cards.title: Habit Stacking & Behaviour Design
cards._legacyHtml:
Habit loop (BJ Fogg / James Clear): Cue → Craving → Routine → Reward. To build new habit: attach to existing behaviour (habit stacking) and make reward immediate.
Habit stacking formula: 'After [CURRENT HABIT], I will [NEW HABIT].' Examples:
• 'After I brush my teeth, I will do 5 minutes of pranayama.'
• 'After I pour my morning coffee, I will take my supplements.'
• 'After my lunch, I will walk for 10 minutes.'
Make it easy (Fogg): Reduce friction for good habits (lay out yoga mat the night before). Increase friction for bad habits (delete social media app, keep phone in another room).
Implementation intention: 'I will [BEHAVIOUR] at [TIME] in [LOCATION].' Doubles follow-through rate vs vague intention (Gollwitzer, 1999).
Tiny habits (start small): Begin so small it feels trivial. 2-minute rule. Build on success not willpower.

cards.title: Patient Compliance — Practical Strategies
cards._legacyHtml:
Barriers to compliance (assess at every visit): Cost, time, social support, beliefs about disease severity, side effects, complexity of regimen, health literacy.
Strategies by barrier:
• Complexity: Simplify regimen. Written action plan. Pill organiser. Habit stack supplements.
• Forgetfulness: Alarm reminders. Pair with daily anchor activity. WhatsApp reminder (set up at appointment).
• Social pressure: Family education session. Involve spouse or family member. Social accountability partner.
• Belief barriers: Explore illness perception model (Leventhal). Address specific misconceptions. Use patient education materials.
• Cost: Prioritise highest-impact interventions. Explore free/low-cost options (walking, lemon water, yoga).
Follow-up structure: 1 week (phone check-in), 2 weeks (in-person review), 1 month (outcome assessment). Document compliance percentage. Celebrate small wins explicitly.

cards.title: Relapse Prevention & Maintenance
cards._legacyHtml:
High-risk situations (identify with patient): Stress, social occasions, travel, illness, negative emotions, boredom. Map the patient's personal triggers.
Coping plans: For each high-risk situation, pre-plan a specific response. 'If I am at a wedding, I will eat before going and choose salad and dal first.'
Lapse vs Relapse: Lapse = one slip. Relapse = return to old pattern. Teach patients: a lapse is not failure. Use coping card.
Coping card (give patient):
• This is a lapse, not a failure.
• I can get back on track right now — not tomorrow.
• One meal/day does not ruin a week's work.
• Call my support person / text the clinic.
Self-monitoring: Food diary, step tracker, weight log — reduces relapse. Review at each visit without judgment.
Booster sessions: Schedule maintenance visits at 1 month, 3 months, 6 months after achieving goal. Prevents drift.
